LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF MARILYN BARTHOLOMEW'S MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT FILED ON AUGUST 20, 2020 IS GRANTED IN PART. THE MOTION IS GRANTED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO THE PRAYER FOR ATTORNEY'S FEES. THE ANSWER DOES NOT PLEAD A CONTRACTUAL, STATUTORY, OR LEGAL BASIS FOR ATTORNEY'S FEES AND DEFENDANTS FAIL TO EXPLAIN HOW THE DEFECT CAN BE CURED BY AMENDMENT. THE MOTION TO STRIKE THE ANSWER IN ITS ENTIRETY IS DENIED. WHETHER THE ANSWER WAS PROPERLY SERVED IS A DISPUTED FACTUAL ISSUE THAT CANNOT BE RESOLVED BY A MOTION TO STRIKE. (SEE CODE CIV. PROC. SEC. 437(A).) A DEFECTIVE PROOF OF SERVICE IS NOT A BASIS TO STRIKE AN ANSWER. MOREOVER, PLAINTIFF ADMITS THAT SHE HAS A COPY OF THE ANSWER SO THIS ASPECT OF THE MOTION IS POINTLESS. (SEE PLAINTIFF'S POINTS & AUTHORITIES, 3:19-20 ["PLAINTIFF OBTAINED THE ANSWER ONLY BY DOWNLOADING IT ON SEPTEMBER 2, 2020 FROM THE COURT'S RECORDS IN THE REGISTER OF ACTIONS FOR THE CASE."].) ORDER SIGNED. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, CLERK: S. LE, NOT REPORTED. =(302/EPS)

DISCOVERY, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE MAY-18-2021 HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF MARILYN BARTHOLOMEW'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES BY DEFENDANT DANIEL STONE TO REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET THREE, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. THE COURT AGREES WITH PLAINTIFF/MOVING PARTY'S REQUEST TO CONTINUE THE HEARING ON THE MOTION WITH AN ADDED DIRECTION TO BOTH SIDES TO CONTINUE THE MEET AND CONFER PROCESS. AS A STARTING POINT AND CONTRIBUTION TO THE MEET AND CONFER PROCESS THE COURT PRELIMINARILY FINDS PRODUCTION OF DEFENDANT'S REAL ESTATE LICENSE AS REASONABLE BUT NO RELEVANCY AS TO PRODUCTION OF DEFENDANT'S EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT WHICH WOULD ALSO BE AN INVASION OF HIS RIGHT OF PRIVACY. THE HEARING ON THE MOTION IS CONTINUED TO JUNE 8, 2021. THE PARTIES SHALL FILE AND SERVE SUPPLEMENTAL BRIEFS ON OR BEFORE 3:00 PM ON JUNE 3, 2021 ADVISING THE COURT OF THE RESULT OF THE CONTINUED MEET AND CONFER PROCESS. JUDGE PRO TEM: STEVEN STEIN; CLERK: SEAN KANE; COURT REPORTER: ROSE MILLER, CSR NO. 5041, EMAIL: RMILLERCSR@GMAIL.COM. (302/JPT)

ON SEPTEMBER 27, 2023, THIS COURT NOTIFIED BARTHOLOMEW THAT SHE MUST SUBMIT AN APPLICATION SEEKING PERMISSION TO APPEAL BY OCTOBER 9. (SEE 391.7, SUBD. (C).) SHE FAILED TO SUBMIT THE REQUIRED APPLICATION. ACCORDINGLY, PERMISSION TO APPEAL IS DENIED. THE APPEAL IS DISMISSED. THE MATTER IS DEEMED COMPLETE IN THIS COURT. (A168668)(SEE SCAN FOR DETAILS)

calendar.matters:
Motion To Vacate The August 14, 2023 Order That Dismissed Defendant John Stewart Company
Off Calendar Nov-14-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: Plaintiff's motion to vacate the August 14, 2023, order that dismissed defendant John Stewart Company.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff's "motion to vacate the August 14, 2023 order that dismissed defendant John Stewart Company" is denied. Plaintiff is a vexatious litigant whose case has now consumed an unprecedented 1,481 entries in this court's register of actions. Her current motion makes three arguments, all unavailing. First, plaintiff argues that this law-and-motion department could not hear the August dismissal motion, because plaintiff had filed other motions in other departments in the past. (Memo. 1:6-11, 18-22.) However, plaintiff cites no authority for this notion, and I know of none. (The inapposite case plaintiff relies on - Rochin v. Pat Johnson Manufacturing Co. (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 1228 - regards a special verdict form.) Second, plaintiff says she was "unprepared" to argue the dismissal motion. (Memo. 1:12-14.) However, plaintiff briefed the motion and concedes that she received three days' advance notice of the tentative ruling granting it, contested that ruling and argued it at hearing. (Bartholomew Dec. 2:12-17.) Third, plaintiff relies on CCP 663 to demand "corrections" in the August "order's statement of facts." (Memo. 1:23-28.) However, CCP 663 regards an "[i]ncorrect or erroneous legal basis for the decision" and plaintiff establishes no legal error.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/RBU)

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF MARILYN BARTHOLOMEW Motion To Vacate The Discovery Order Dated June 12, 2023 That Granted Defendant Golden Gate Tow, Inc. Discovery Motion For Terminating Sanctions Against Plaintiff

Argument is heard. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with a slight variation. The motion is denied. The relief requested is not supported by mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect, as those terms are meant and used in statute and case law. The variation mentioned is the Motion is denied without prejudice to allow the Plaintiff to refile if she so chooses. The court finds no merits in the matter and cautions that the Plaintiff must appear at the next court date if she chooses to refile.

Judge Pro Tem: John-Paul Deol; Clerk: R. Sahatjian. Court Reporter: Robert Balian CSR# email:rbalian@sft.org. (D506)

Motion To Dismiss The Case Against Defendant John Stewart Company
Off Calendar Aug-14-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom Conference
Marilyn Bartholomew, (707) 236-4112, for Plaintiff in Pro Per.
Nick Reckas, Esq., LAW OFFICES OF NICK RECKAS, 415-464-0913. for Defendants Golden Gate Tow, Inc., The John Stewart Company, Daniel Ingram Stone Real Estate Broker dba Cornerstone Real Estate Services LLC.
Ann Delgado CSR#11185, adelgado@sftc.org, Court reporter,

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument, adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant John Stewart Company's motion to dismiss the case against it is granted.

Defendant's motion is pursuant to CCP 583.410, CCP 583.420 and CRC 3.1342 on grounds that three years have passed since the case was initiated against defendant and plaintiff has failed to bring the case to trial.

The court may in its discretion dismiss an action for delay in prosecution on its own motion or on motion of the defendant if to do so appears to the court appropriate under the circumstances of the case. (CCP 583.410.)

Plaintiff opposes to the motion to dismiss on two grounds: 1) motion must be heard by Judge Massullo, and not in Department 302 Law and Motion and 2) only Judge Massullo may determine whether plaintiff is exempt from the three-year timeline to bring the case to trial.

First, plaintiff is incorrect in her contention that Judge Massullo should hear the motion. Per Local Rule 8.1 pretrial civil motions are heard in the Law and Motion Department.

Second, plaintiff argues that Judge Massullo is the only judge who has received all the information needed to decide that plaintiff is not at fault for the delay. (See Opp. 3:22-26; Batholomew Decl., 2:11-15.) Plaintiff states that it is the court that is at fault, because a clerk did not complete the entry of default for defendant Mercedes Gavin. (Id.) However, plaintiff was ordered to appear on May 9, 2023, to show cause why the action should not be dismissed or why sanctions should not be imposed for failure to file proof of service and obtain answer or enter default against defendant Gavin. (See Judge Massullo's March 15, 2023 Order.) On May 9, 2023, plaintiff made no appearance and filed no objection and Gavin was dismissed pursuant to CCP 583.250 and for failure to comply with CCP 583.210. (Judge Massullo's May 10, 2023 Order.) Thus, Judge Massullo made the determination that plaintiff failed to properly serve Gavin or enter default against her, and plaintiff did not appear or object. Much of the delay in prosecuting this case resulted from plaintiff adding defendant Gavin to the case in the third amended complaint on December 26, 2019, and then failing to serve her for nearly four years.

The court has taken into consideration all the of factors under CRC 3.1342, and with the guidance of the policies set forth in CCP 583.130, dismisses the case for delay in prosecution, as it has been more than three years since the action has commenced. (CCP 583.420.)

Counsel for prevailing party, Nick Reckas, Esq., is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling pursuant to CRC3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Ann Delgado CSR#11185, adelgado@sftc.org, Court reporter, , Reported. (302/RBU)

LAW AND MOTION
Off Calendar Aug-14-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom Conference
Marilyn Bartholomew, (707) 236-4112, for Plaintiff in Pro Per.
Nick Reckas, Esq., LAW OFFICES OF NICK RECKAS, 415-464-0913. for Defendants Golden Gate Tow, Inc., The John Stewart Company, Daniel Ingram Stone Real Estate Broker dba Cornerstone Real Estate Services LLC.
Ann Delgado CSR#11185, adelgado@sftc.org, Court reporter,

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument, adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF MARILYN BARTHOLOMEW Motion For An Order Vacating The Order Of Dismissal Dated May 10, 2023 That Dismissed Defendant Mercedes A. Gavin, And For An Order Confirming That The Summons And Third Amended Complaint Were Served Timely And Properly Upon This Defendant

Plaintiff's motion to vacate the May 10, 2023, order of dismissal is denied.

Plaintiff argues that she is entitled to relief under CCP 473(b) because she was subject to surprise by the court's May 10, 2023, order. (Mot., 6:4-7.) Plaintiff contends that the court did not consider her April 19, 2023, filing of responsive papers in its ruling. (Id.) However, plaintiff was ordered by the court to appear on May 9, 2023, to show cause as to why defendant Gavin should not be dismissed, and plaintiff chose not to appear and disregarded the order because she "need(ed) at least 8 hours sleep." (Opp., 3:1-3.) The court needed to hear from plaintiff and plaintiff chose not to appear or file a formal objection with sufficient information, prior to the hearing. Therefore, plaintiff was not subject to any surprise.

Plaintiff next argues that she is entitled to relief under CCP 473(d) because under CCP 581(d) the May 10, 2023, order is voidable or void, because it is unsigned. (Opp., 7:23-27.) The order was electronically signed by Judge Massullo. (CRC 2.2579(e) ["If a document requires a signature by a court or a judicial officer, the document may be electronically signed in any manner permitted by law."].)

Counsel for prevailing party, Nick Reckas, Esq., is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling pursuant to CRC3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Ann Delgado CSR#11185, adelgado@sftc.org, Court reporter, , Reported. (302/RBU)

MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Marilyn Bartholomew, 707-236-4112 Plaintiff in Pro Per.
Nick T. Reckas, Esq. 415-464-0913 for Defendant Golden Gate Tow..
Court reporter: Rocio Lopez, CSR #11194, 415-337-1537, Rlopez@sftc.org, Reported.

RULING - A R G U E D.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

MOTION PURSUANT TO CCP SECTIONS 473(B), 473(D), AND 1008(D), FOR AN ORDER REVOKING, SETTING ASIDE, OR VACATING THE VOIDABLE ORDER THE COURT ISSUED ON DECEMBER 12, 2022 IN EXCESS OF THE COURT JURISDICTION

Plaintiff's "motion for relief pursuant to CCP sections 473(b), 473(d) and 1008(d), for an order revoking, setting aside or vacating the voidable order the court issued on December 12, 2022 in excess of the court's jurisdiction" is denied. The motion argues 13 grounds; all are unavailing and most have been raised and rejected before. (Not. 2:17-4:10.)

Grounds 1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10 are based on the notion that defendant Dan Stone's filing of a renewed vexatious litigant motion was surprising and improper. It was not. As here, "a subsequent application for the same order may be made upon new or different facts." (CCP 1008(b); see also 12/12/22 Order 1:24-2:4.)

Grounds 4 and 5 argue that Stone's renewed vexatious litigant motion had no legal basis. Not so. (12/12/22 Order 1:26 ["Defendant Stone's motion is for renewal under CCP 1008(b)"].)
Grounds 11, 12 and 13 argue that "failing to make an official court reporter available for a trial proceeding" is error. This argument is not jurisdictional and the case is not in trial. In any event, court reporters are being made available in plaintiff's hearings.

Mr. Reckas to submit a proposed form of order to the Court. ***

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Court reporter: Rocio Lopez, CSR #11194, 415-337-1537, Rlopez@sftc.org, Reported. (302/RBU)

ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Nov-22-2021 Continued To Mar-15-22 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DISCOVERY
Off Calendar Nov-10-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
No appearance by plaintiff/cross-defendant Marilyn Bartholomew;
Nick T. Reckas, Esq., Tel. 415.464.0913, appears via ZOOM videoconference for defendant/cross-complainant Golden Gate Tow.

Matter called. The Court states that it was not clear from Marilyn Bartholomew's single communication to the court about the tentative ruling whether she contested it, and she did not respond to a request for clarification posed by Golden Gate Tow's counsel. Mr. Reckas confirms that he did not receive a response to his email from the plaintiff. The matter is passed by the Court.

Second call of the matter at 9:16 a.m., the Plaintiff is not present.

There being no other appearance, the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE TOW, INC.'s Motion To Compel Responses To Defendant's Request For Production Of Documents, Set One And Form Interrogatories is adopted as follows:

Golden Gate Tow's Motion is DENIED in part and GRANTED in part.

1) Form Interrogatory (Set One) No. 15.1: Ms. Bartholomew served her interrogatory answers before she filed her answer to Golden Gate Tow's cross-complaint. Consequently, it was premature for her to answer No. 15.1. Moreover, Ms. Bartholomew has no duty to update her earlier answers to this interrogatory, and "continuing" interrogatories are not allowed. Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 2030.060(g). This finding is without prejudice to Golden Gate Tow's ability to re-propound interrogatory No. 15.1 as if for the first time.

2) Request for Production (Set One) No. 6: It appears clear from the court's own file, including prior papers filed by Ms. Bartholomew, that this inspection demand reasonably refers to the April 30, 2018 order which references and summarizes the parties' settlement agreement. Ms. Bartholomew's objections are overruled. The Court directs her to serve a verified code-compliant amended response within 30 days of the date of this Order. Said response must be without objections, including ones based on privilege or the protection of attorney work product.

The term "code-compliant" means in compliance with the California Civil Discovery Act, Code of Civil Procedure sections 2016.010 et seq., as interpreted by the decisions of the courts of this state. For example, in preparing her amended responses, Ms. Bartholomew should be mindful that, unless otherwise limited by order of the court, any party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 2017.010.

Ms. Bartholomew is also admonished that sanctionable misuses of the discovery process include: (1) making, without substantial justification, an unmeritorious objection to discovery; (2) making an evasive response to discovery; and (3) making or opposing, unsuccessfully and without substantial justification, a motion to compel discovery. Code Civ. Proc. Sections 2023.010, 2023.030.

Golden Gate Tow's request for monetary sanctions is DENIED.

Mr. Reckas is to submit a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and that complies with CRC 3.1312. The proposed order is to be submitted to Judge Pro Tem Vestal in Word format at: PVestal@nvlawllp.com.

The court reporter was released following the Court's ruling after the second call of the matter. There was no appearance by the plaintiff by the time that the court's discovery calendar concluded just prior to 9:30 a.m.

(Amended) Re-Notice Of Hearing For Demurrer By Cross-Defendant Marilyn A. Bartholomew To Cross-Complaint By Golden Gate Tow, Inc.
Off Calendar Aug-03-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF MARILYN BARTHOLOMEW'S (Amended) Re-Notice Of Hearing For Demurrer To Cross-Complaint By Golden Gate Tow, Inc.:

Cross-Defendant Ms. Bartholomew's demurrer to the cross-complaint is overruled in its entirety. The cross-complaint asserts one cause of action against Ms. Bartholomew for breach of contract. The cross-complaint alleges Ms. Bartholomew breached the parties' written settlement agreement. (Cross-Complaint pars. 1-2, 5.) The doctrine of res judicata does not apply. The alleged breach of the settlement agreement has not litigated or decided on the merits. (See Boeken v. Philip Morris USA, Inc. (2010) 48 Cal.4th 788, 797-98.) Nor does the doctrine of judicial estoppel apply. (Swahn Group, Inc. v. Segal (2010) 183 Cal.App.4th 831, 844 ["The trial court may sustain a demurrer on the ground of judicial estoppel where the facts pleaded and judicially noticed indicate as a matter of law the doctrine should be applied, i.e., that a court has adopted or accepted as true the inconsistent position."].) The cross-complaint alleges cross-complainant filed a small claims action against Ms. Bartholomew, but rather than litigate the matter, the parties settled. (Cross-Complaint 1.) There are no such inconsistent positions.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Revoking And Or Setting Aside A Stipulation Order And A Discovery Order; ***Court Reporter Fees Paid***
Off Calendar May-25-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Marilyn Bartholomew (707-236-4112; wildcatpeak@yahoo.com), plaintiff in propria persona.
No other appearances.

Discussed. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF MARILYN BARTHOLOMEW's Motion For An Order Revoking And Or Setting Aside A Stipulation Order And A Discovery Order:

Motion GRANTED in part as follows. The tentative ruling that I transmitted to the Clerk for the hearing on April 13, 2021, included "hearing required," but that language was not included in the online tentative ruling system. This omission was not brought to my attention prior to issuing the Order filed on April 16, 2021. Judicial officers must provide parties the full right to be heard according to law, and there is a difference between a party failing to appear after being ordered to appear (what I thought happened) and a party choosing not to appear when there was no such order (what actually happened). Therefore, the request to vacate the Order filed on April 16, 2021 is GRANTED. CCP 473, 1008. Prior to the hearing on the motion for reconsideration, the parties should meet and confer to determine whether they will argue the underlying motion at the hearing on May 25. If they agree to do so, I will hear argument on that motion. If they do not agree to do so, the parties should be prepared to discuss scheduling a new hearing on the underlying motion. I do not have authority to grant or deny a request to withdraw the stipulation to me acting as judge pro tem; such a motion "must" be heard by the Presiding Judge or his designee. CRC 2.816(e).

The underlying motion is reset for June 23, 2021 at 9:00am, discovery dept. Parties may meet and confer in compliance with SFLR 8.2B for a continuance date for this hearing to a specially setting time if needed.

Order is signed.

Judge Pro Tem: Scott Borrowman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR#5041, email: RMillerCSR@gmail.com . =(302/JPT)

MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER to 3RD Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-19-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF MARILYN BARTHOLOMEW's MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER to 3RD Amended COMPLAINT:

Plaintiff's motion to strike the answer to the Third Amended Complaint filed on August 20, 2020 is granted in part. The motion is granted without leave to amend as to the prayer for attorney's fees. The answer does not plead a contractual, statutory, or legal basis for attorney's fees and defendants fail to explain how the defect can be cured by amendment. The motion to strike the answer in its entirety is denied. Whether the answer was properly served is a disputed factual issue that cannot be resolved by a motion to strike. (See Code Civ. Proc. sec. 437(a).) A defective proof of service is not a basis to strike an answer. Moreover, plaintiff admits that she has a copy of the answer so this aspect of the motion is pointless. (See Plaintiff's Points & Authorities, 3:19-20 ["Plaintiff obtained the Answer only by downloading it on September 2, 2020 from the court's records in the Register of Actions for the case."].)

order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Motion To Enter A Prefiling Order Against Plaintiff On The Grounds She Is A Vexatious Litigant And To Require That She Post Security To Continue With The Lawsuit
Off Calendar Sep-29-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Marilyn Bartholomew (707-236-4112; wildcatpeak@yahoo.com) Plaintiff in propria persona.
No other appearances.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT DANIEL STONE's Motion To Enter A Prefiling Order Against Plaintiff On The Grounds She Is A Vexatious Litigant And To Require That She Post Security To Continue With The Lawsuit:

Defendant Stone's motion for a pre-filing order under Code of Civil Procedure section 391(b)(1) [five dismissals over previous seven years] is denied. The case that Stone identifies as "case 4" was brought against plaintiff. (Stone's RJN, Exs. 7 & 8.) The pre-filing requirements apply to self-represented plaintiffs and in case 4, plaintiff was a self-represented defendant appealing from an adverse judgment in litigation she did not initiate. Case 4, therefore, does not count against plaintiff. (See John v. Superior Court (2016) 63 Cal.4th 91, 98 ["It is clear the Legislature never intended courts to redefine the term 'plaintiff' to include all appellants, regardless of their role in the trial court action."].) In recognition of this defect, Stone attempts to introduce a new case on reply for the court's consideration. The court refuses to allow that tactic because new evidence cannot be introduced on reply. (See Jay v. Mahaffey (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 1522, 1537-1538 ["The general rule of motion practice, which applies here, is that new evidence is not permitted with reply papers. . . . The inclusion of additional evidentiary matter with the reply should only be allowed in the exceptional case . . . ."].)

Stone's motion for a prefiling order under Code of Civil Procedure section 391(b)(2) [repeatedly relitigates after final determination] is denied. Stone merely relies on the docket in "case 3" (Case No. 3:2018cv07478) for this argument. (Stone's RJN, Ex. 10.) The docket shows that plaintiff took a frivolous appeal, but it does not show that she repeatedly relitigated the case. Moreover, Stone was not a party to that proceeding and none of the claims pertains to him. The cited subdivision is therefore inapposite.

Stone's motion for a prefiling order under Code of Civil Procedure section 391(b)(3) [repeated unmeritorious filings] is denied. The fact that plaintiff has filed several amended complaints and engaged in robust discovery fails to demonstrate a basis for this request.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Sep-29-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Marilyn Bartholomew (707-236-4112; wildcatpeak@yahoo.com) Plaintiff in propria persona.
No other appearances.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT MERCEDES GAVIN's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS:

Defendant Gavin's motion to quash is granted. When a defendant contests jurisdiction, plaintiff has the burden of proof to establish a basis for jurisdiction. (See Mihlon v. Superior Court (1985) 169 Cal.App.3d 703, 710.) Here, the undisputed evidence fails to show proper service. Gavin declares that she never received a summons that named her. (Gavin Decl., par. 2.) Plaintiff could not have effectuated substitute service on 5/24/19 because even if plaintiff served the amended summons on that date, the only inference is that plaintiff did not exercise due diligence before doing so because the amended summons was issued on the same date. (Gavin's RJN, Exs 1-3.) The court denies plaintiff's request for a continuance. Plaintiff's contemporaneous preparation of a motion to strike belies her assertion that she was too busy to oppose this motion. The court also cannot imagine-given the record- what evidence plaintiff could proffer that could change the outcome of this motion.

Counsel for Gavin is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the adopted ruling.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

MOTION TO STRIKE 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-16-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE TOW, INC., THE JOHN STEWART COMPANY, DANIEL STONE MOTION TO STRIKE 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT

No appearances made by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

Defendants' motion to strike is denied in its entirety. This court rejects Plaintiff's argument that this demurrer is untimely. On February 26, 2020, this court took Defendants' demurrer off calendar for failure to comply with their obligation to meet and confer under Code Civ. Proc. 431.40, and gave Defendants until March 26 to file their responsive pleadings. As a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, however, the Presiding Judge's General Order deemed the period from March 18 through June 1 to be court holidays for purposes of calculating deadlines. Further, Code of Civ. Proc. "section 473, subdivision (a)(1) allows the court to increase the time for filing a demurrer in furtherance of justice and on any terms that may be proper. A trial court's consideration of a late-filed demurrer does not affect plaintiff's "substantial rights," where plaintiff did not take steps to obtain a default judgment or demonstrate the delay prejudiced her. (Jackson v. Doe (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 742, 750.) This court chooses to exercise its discretion to do so. In addition, rather than further delay a ruling on the issues in the demurrer and motion to strike on the basis of the meet and confer disagreements, the Court sees fit to issue a decision on the merits based on the briefing previously submitted. "We emphasize that such use of the motion to strike should be cautious and sparing. We have no intention of creating a procedural "line item veto" for the civil defendant." (PH II, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1680, 1683.) Defendants fail to show that any of its contested allegations are improper, irrelevant, or false on the face of the complaint, per Code of Civ. Proc. 436(a).

Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the ruling verbatim.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR#5041, email: RMillerCSR@gmail.com (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-16-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE TOW, INC., THE JOHN STEWART COMPANY, DANIEL STONE DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT

No appearances made by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

Defendants' demurrer is overruled as to the third and fourth cause of action and sustained without leave to amend as to the fifth cause of action. This court rejects Plaintiff's argument that this demurrer is untimely. On February 26, 2020, this court took Defendants' demurrer off calendar for failure to comply with the meet and confer rules under the Code of Civ. Proc, and gave Defendants until March 26 to file their responsive pleadings. As a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, however, the Presiding Judge's General Order deemed the period from March 18 through June 1 to be court holidays for purposes of calculating deadlines. Further, Code of Civ. Proc. section 473(a)(1) allows the court to increase the time for filing a demurrer in furtherance of justice and on any terms that may be proper. A trial court's consideration of a late-filed demurrer does not affect plaintiff's "substantial rights," where plaintiff did not take steps to obtain a default judgment or demonstrate the delay prejudiced her. (Jackson v. Doe (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 742, 750.) This court chooses to exercise its discretion to do so. In addition, rather than further delay a ruling on the issues in the demurrer and motion to strike on the basis of the meet and confer disagreements, the Court sees fit to issue a decision on the merits based on the briefing previously submitted. Defendants' demurrer to the third cause of action under CVC 22658 is overruled. Plaintiff's allegations sufficiently state a cause of action against Stone and JSCO. (See TAC pars. 7, 10, 20.) Defendants' arguments as to the allegations against GGTOW are rejected. (See Daniels v. Select Portfolio Servicing, Inc. (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1150, 1167 ["[A] general demurrer may not be sustained, nor a motion for judgment on the pleadings granted, as to a portion of a cause of action."].) Defendants' demurrer to the fourth cause of action for negligence is overruled. A "party demurring to a pleading that has been amended after a demurrer to an earlier version of the pleading was sustained shall not demur to any portion of the amended complaint, cross-complaint, or answer on grounds that could have been raised by demurrer to the earlier version of the complaint, cross-complaint, or answer." (Code Civ. Proc. 430.41(b).) Defendants did not demur to the cause of action for negligence in their July 2019 demurrer to the first amended complaint in July 2019, and will not be permitted now to raise those arguments. Defendants' demurrer to the fifth cause of action for IIED is sustained without leave to amend. As explained in the court's order last July, Plaintiff has not alleged conduct that is extreme and outrageous as a matter of law. (Plotnik v. Meihause (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 1590, 1614.) Despite Plaintiff's amendments, the allegations still fail to go beyond the annoyance and inconvenience of deceptive car towing, and do not form the basis for an IIED claim. (See Yurick v. Superior Court (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1116, 1128 ["ordinary defendants are not liable for mere insult, indignity, annoyance, or even threats, where the case is lacking other circumstances of aggravation."]; Davidson v. City of Westminster (1982) 32 Cal.3d 197, 209 [outrageous conduct must be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community"].)

Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the ruling verbatim.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR#5041, email: RMillerCSR@gmail.com (302/EPS)

Plaintiff Marilyn Bartholomew's motion to file a third amended complaint is granted. Ms. Bartholomew is granted leave to file her third amended complaint no later than December 27, 2019. Defendants waived their insufficient service objection by opposing the motion on its merits. (Carlton v. Quint (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 690, 697). California has a strong public policy of allowing amendments to pleadings absent legal prejudice to the opposing parties. Defendants have not identified any legal prejudice to them if this motion is granted. Defendants' arguments based on insufficiency of the claims alleged in the third amended complaint are best addressed on a pleading motion such as a demurrer rather than this motion.

Off Calendar Aug-06-2019 Continued To Oct-23-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Defendants' Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiff'S First Amended Complaint (Filed May-20-2019)
Off Calendar Jul-25-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendants' Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiff'S First Amended Complaint (Filed May-20-2019)

Defendants Golden Gate Tow Inc., The John Stewart Company, and Daniel Ingram Stone dba Cornerstone Real Estate Services, LLC's motion to strike portions of Plaintiff Marilyn Bartholomew's first amended complaint is granted in part with 30 days leave to amend. Although the parties continue to contest whether they have met and conferred as required, rather than further delay a ruling on the sufficiency of the operative pleading, the Court sees fit to issue a decision on the merits based on the briefing previously submitted. Although Plaintiff states that she intends to amend her complaint, she did not move for leave to do so. Instead, the Court will liberally allow amendments to her complaint to cure allegations subject to this motion to strike. (See Vaccaro v. Kaiman (1998) 63 Cal.App.4th 761, 768 ["When the defect which justifies striking a complaint is capable of cure, the court should allow leave to amend."].)

The allegations at paragraphs 57, 60, 63, and 67 pertaining to calculations of damages for violations of Vehicle Code section 22658 are improper and are stricken. The plain language of the statute suggests that damages are calculated based on charges actually assessed, not based on hypothetical calculations. (Veh. Code 22658(e)(1), (l)(5), (m)(3).) Plaintiff has leave to amend these allegations if she can allege in good faith that she was subjected to towing and storage charges under the relevant statutory provisions. Plaintiff should not allege hypothetical amounts of damages, but money she was actually required to pay.

The attorneys' fees request in the prayer for relief is improper because there is no allegation that forms a basis for it in contract or statute. (See Code Civ. Proc. 1021 ["Except as attorney's fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties"].) Plaintiff misapplies the tort-of-another doctrine. Beraksa v. Stardust Records, Inc. (1963) 215 Cal.App.2d 708 relied upon Civil Code section 3318 involving breach of warranty of an agent's authority which provides for "reasonable expenses of legal proceedings taken, in good faith, to enforce the act of the agent against his principal." Moreover, self-represented litigants usually cannot recover attorneys' fees. (See Trope v. Katz (1995) 11 Cal.4th 274, 285, 292 [holding that pro se litigants cannot recover reasonable attorney's fees under Civil Code section 1717].) Plaintiff has leave to amend if she can allege in good faith that there is a contractual or statutory basis for attorneys' fees.

The motion to strike paragraphs 6, 13, 15, 33, 45, and 68 of the complaint is denied. Those allegations contain potentially relevant background information, and are not "irrelevant, false, or improper matter" subject to being stricken under Code of Civil Procedure Section 436.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE TOW, INC., THE JOHN STEWART COMPANY, DANIEL STONE's DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF'S 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Defendants Golden Gate Tow Inc., The John Stewart Company, and Daniel Ingram Stone dba Cornerstone Real Estate Services, LLC's demurrer to Plaintiff Marilyn Bartholomew's first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained as to each of the five causes of action with 30 days leave to amend. Although the parties continue to contest whether they have met and conferred as required, rather than further delay a ruling on the sufficiency of the operative pleading, the Court sees fit to issue a decision on the merits based on the briefing previously submitted. Although Plaintiff states that she intends to amend her complaint, she did not move for leave to do so. Regardless, the Court, in sustaining Defendants' demurrer, will adhere to the liberal policy of allowing amendments to cure defects. (See Aubry v. Tri-City Hospital Dist. (1992) 2 Cal.4th 962, 970-971 ["Where the complaint is defective, in the furtherance of justice great liberality should be exercised in permitting a plaintiff to amend his complaint, and it ordinarily constitutes an abuse of discretion to sustain a demurrer without leave to amend if there is a reasonable possibility that the defect can be cured by amendment."] [internal quotations omitted].)

Although Counsel for Defendants produced a declaration purporting to satisfy her meet and confer obligations, Plaintiff has filed a declaration denying meet and confer occurred. MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-11-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Marilyn Bartholomew (707-236-4112) Plaintiff in propria persona via Court Call.
Nick T. Reckas, esq. (415-464-0913) specially appearing for attorney Mercedes Gavin for Defendants.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE TOW, INC.'s special MOTION TO STRIKE and dismiss the first, second, and fifth cause of action of plaintiff's 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT

Defendant Golden Gate Tow Inc.'s special motion to strike the first, second, and fifth causes of action of Plaintiff Marilyn Bartholomew's first amended complaint (FAC) for fraud, conspiracy to defraud, and intentional infliction of emotional distress is denied. Golden Gate Tow has not met its burden under the first prong of the anti-SLAPP analysis. In order for a claim to be subject to the anti-SLAPP statute, the claim must arise from any written or oral statement or writing made in connection with an issue under consideration or review by a judicial body. (Code Civ. Proc. 425.16(e)(2); Rand Resources, LLC v. City of Carson (2019) 6 Cal.5th 610, 623.) Where a cause of action alleges both protected and unprotected activity, the cause of action will not be subject to the anti-SLAPP statute if the protected conduct is merely incidental to the unprotected conduct. (Philipson & Simon v. Gulsvig (2007) 154 Cal.App.4th 347, 358-359.) "[T]he mere fact that an action was filed after protected activity took place does not mean the action arose from that activity for the purposes of the anti-SLAPP statute." (Navellier v. Sletten (2002) 29 Cal.4th 82, 89.)

Plaintiff's FAC references Small Claims Court Case No. CSM-18-857224 and the surrounding negotiations over the return of Plaintiff's towed vehicle, but these allegations are included primarily for context and need not be relied upon by the three causes of action at issue on this motion. (FAC 37, 49, 51-52.) The gravamen of these causes of action against Golden Gate Tow are seizure of Plaintiff's vehicle without a valid basis for doing so, falsely representing that the vehicle would be returned to her free of charge, and then manufacturing a parking violation that Golden Gate Tow could use to continue holding her vehicle and extract money of out her. (FAC 17, 19, 27, 30, 31, 33, 34, 36, 70-73, 76-79, 84-86, 107, 109-110; see Haight Ashbury Free Clinics, Inc. v. Happening House Ventures (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 1539, 1550 ["In general, whether a cause of action is subject to a motion to strike under the SLAPP statute turns on whether the gravamen of the cause of action targets protected activity."].) These allegations of fraud and infliction of emotional distress primarily relate to fraudulent activity before the small claims action was instituted or, with regard to the allegedly fraudulent invoice, after the settlement agreement was reached. (Id.) The allegations about emails providing false evidence are primarily made against Defendant Mercedes Gavin, not against Golden Gate Tow, and thus have no bearing on this analysis. (FAC 39, 40, 43, 44, 108.)

Order is signed in open court.
